data.title: Trump appears to have posted some jobs data early
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump posted a chart on his social media account late Thursday that included job-market data that was not publicly released until Friday morning.

The chart, which has been liked nearly 14,000 times by other Truth Social users, showed the economy added 654,000 private-sector jobs since January, and had 181,000 fewer government jobs, figures that were only published by the Labor Department on Friday in its closely-watched December jobs report. The president typically gets the jobs report a day early. Bloomberg News reported the apparent breach earlier.

The White House did not immediately respond to a request for comment. Trump's posted chart cited the Labor Department's Bureau of Labor Statistics and calculations by the Council of Economic Advisers as its sources.

data.title: Soybeans Rise Despite Worries About Elevated Supply — Market Talk
data.description: Soybeans recover some ground while markets worry about elevated global supplies. A Supreme Court ruling on tariffs fails to materialize, allowing traders to focus on Monday's upgrade of USDA projections. "It seems that the market is now more concerned about big supplies coming soon from South America with the Soybeans harvest there just weeks away," Price Group's Jack Scoville writes. He adds that U.S. prices "are currently too high to complete many new sales." There are also talks that Brazilian soy growers could be holding on to their crops waiting for better prices. The most-active soybeans contract rises 0.5%, while corn and wheat both fall slightly. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

data.title: Paying for Trump's "Dream Military" Could Be a Nightmare — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Desmond Lachman

About the author: Desmond Lachman is a senior fellow at the American Enterprise Institute. He was a deputy director in the International Monetary Fund's Policy Development and Review Department and the chief emerging market economic strategist at Salomon Smith Barney.

Judging by President Donald Trump's announcement Wednesday that he wants to increase defense spending from the current annual rate of $901 billion to $1.5 trillion by 2027, it seems the president has forgotten his U.S. history. He has blotted out how a massive increase in defense spending for the Vietnam War ignited the Great Inflation of the 1970s. This makes it all too likely that the U.S. will again experience another inflationary burst, should Trump get his way.

From 1965 to 1967, President Lyndon Johnson increased U.S. defense spending by around 30%, from $58 billion to $75 billion a year. He did so without raising taxes to pay for that increased spending. As a result, the U.S. swung from a near balanced budget in 1965 to a deficit of almost 3% of gross domestic product in 1968. The economy overheated. Inflation jumped 4%, hitting 5.5% by 1969.

Fast forward to today. After inheriting a deficit of more than 6% of GDP and a public debt level of 100% of GDP, Trump further compromised the country's public finances with his One Big Beautiful Bill Act. The Congressional Budget Office estimates that over the next decade that legislation will add around $3.4 trillion to the deficit. The International Monetary Fund now estimates that the deficit will remain around 7% of GDP for as far as the eye can see. That would take the debt-to-GDP ratio to a Greek or Italian-like 130% by 2034.

Undeterred by the parlous state of the country's finances and by stubbornly overheated inflation, Trump is now proposing a more than 50% increase in defense spending to allow the U.S. to build a "Dream Military." If he gets his way, defense spending would increase by around $5 trillion through 2035. Add on top of that $800 billion in additional interest.

Trump suggested that tariff revenue will pay for this. There are a number of obvious weaknesses to that idea.

The first is that the expected income from tariffs over the next decade is only $2.5 trillion to $3 trillion, or around half of Trump's proposed increase in defense spending, according to the CBO. The second is that there is a reasonable likelihood that the Supreme Court will very soon strike down the tariffs by ruling that Trump exceeded his authority in invoking national security reasons to raise a large portion of his tariff revenue. And finally, Trump has said tariff revenue should be used to give a $2,000 check to most Americans. The total cost of those checks would exceed the expected tariff revenue by a large margin, leaving nothing to cover the increased defense spending.

The bottom line is that even without Trump's proposed increase in defense spending, the U.S. government will need to raise $2 trillion a year on a net basis for the foreseeable future. That amount would be considerably higher should Trump get his way on an expanded defense budget.

None of this bodes well for inflation, the bond market, or the dollar. As was the case with Johnson's budget deficit in the 1960s, the expanded Trump budget deficit could renew economic overheating. That would especially be the case if Trump continues to push the Federal Reserve to drastically lower interest rates and if he installs a monetary policy dove as Fed chair in May.

That would mount pressure on Treasury bond market yields and the dollar, especially if investors come to fear that Trump is trying to inflate his way out from under the public debt mountain.

We just have to hope that, unlike Trump, Congress remembers the past. Maybe Congress will recall the painful inflation that Johnson's Vietnam War budget unleashed on the American people and do the responsible thing today: to only approve Trump's defense proposal if it is accompanied by tax increases and nondefense spending cuts.

Guest commentaries like this one are written by authors outside the Barron's newsroom. They reflect the perspective and opinions of the authors. Submit feedback and commentary pitches to ideas@barrons.com .

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Partly cloudy payrolls: Weak job adds, strong wages, lower jobless rate, dipping participation
data.description:
Key points:
  • Main US indexes advance; Nasdaq out front, up ~0.6%
  • Utilities lead S&P sector gainers; Healthcare down most
  • Euro STOXX 600 index up ~1%
  • Dollar, gold rise; crude up >3%; bitcoin ~flat;
  • US 10-Year Treasury yield edges down to ~4.17%

PARTLY CLOUDY PAYROLLS: WEAK JOBS, STRONG WAGES, LOWER JOBLESS RATE, DIPPING PARTICIPATION

The Labor Department delivered a ho-hum December employment report on Friday.

The U.S. economy added 50,000 jobs last month (USNFAR=ECI), a 10.7% drop from November's downwardly revised 56,000 increase, and 10,000 fewer than analysts expected.

It was the ninth payrolls number in 2025 that surprised to the downside.

A peek under the hood shows service providers accounted for more than 100% of private sector job adds, with goods producers, construction, manufacturing and retail all shedding between 8,000 and 25,000 jobs.

Government payrolls increased by 13,000.

Whether this report supports the rationale for a Fed rate cut in the near future is a matter of some debate.

Peter Cardillo, chief market economist at Spartan Capital Securities tells Reuters, “The average (monthly payroll growth) is probably going to be closer to maybe 30,000 in the quarter, and that should give ample room for the Fed to cut."

On the other hand, Seema Shah, chief global strategist at Principal Asset Management says, "the prospect of a January Fed rate cut has all but vanished following the unexpected drop in the unemployment rate."

Speaking of which, the unemployment rate (USUNR=ECI) delivered another surprise, falling to 4.4%. Consensus called for a reading of 4.5%, a repeat of November's downwardly revised number.

That can at least partly be attributed to the Labor Market Participation rate, which ticked 0.1 percentage point lower, to 62.4%; when workers leave the labor market, they are no longer considered unemployed.

"Participation remains soft, pipelines are thin, and confidence is slipping," writes Nigel Green, CEO of deVere Group.

Wage growth, providing a first glimpse of December inflation, rose to 0.3% from 0.2%, as economists predicted.

Year-over-year, however, average hourly wages grew by 3.8%, hotter than the 3.6% consensus.

On the bright side, the Labor Department is due to release its Consumer Price Index (CPI) Tuesday, and year-over-year core CPI is seen printing at 2.7%, which would put real earnings growth at 1.1%.

That means the consumer has more spending money, which should put them in a good mood.

Speaking of the mood of the consumer, who carries the burden of about 70% of the U.S. economy, it brightened this month.

The University of Michigan's (UMich) preliminary take on January Consumer Sentiment (USUMSP=ECI) rose to 54.0, a 2.1% monthly improvement and a stronger reading than the 53.5 economists predicted.

Survey participants' assessment of current conditions improved by 4.0%, while near-term expectations increased by a more sedate 0.7%.

"Consumer sentiment inched up for the second straight month and reached its highest reading since September 2025," says Joanne Hsu, director of UMich's Consumer Surveys. "Although consumers’ worries about tariffs appear to be gradually receding, they remain guarded about the overall strength of business conditions and labor markets."

Near- and long-term inflation expectations are elevated and stalled.

Respondents see annual inflation at 4.2% 12 months from now, unchanged from the previous reading, and 2.3 percentage points hotter than 2.7%, or Wednesday's core CPI reading, but 1.5 percentage points hotter than next week's anticipated core CPI print.

Five-year inflation expectations heated up from 3.4% to 3.6%.

Turning to the housing market, in a stale report delayed by the government shutdown, groundbreaking on new American homes (USHST=ECI) slid by 4.6% in October to 1.246 million units at a seasonally adjusted, annualized rate (SAAR), according to the Commerce Department.

That's 6.0% weaker than the 1.325 million units SAAR analysts were expecting.

The weakness, however, was largely driven by the volatile multi-unit segment. Single-family housing starts actually rebounded, rising 5.4%.

"The apparently stabilization in new single-family construction looks encouraging," says Oliver Allen, senior U.S. economist at Pantheon Macroeconomics. "But the sector still is far from out of the woods."

Building permits (USBPE=ECI) - considered one of the housing market's more forward-looking indicators - essentially moved sideways, inching a meager 0.2% lower to 1.412 million units SAAR, but still beat consensus by 4.6%.

data.title: After a Tough 2025, Lockheed Martin Stock Can Have a Better 2026 — Barrons.com
data.description:
Al Root

Shares of defense prime contractor Lockheed Martin have underperformed peers as fears about its key F-35 program, losses in its classified business, and shifting U.S. defense policies have kept investors on the sidelines.

Most of that was in 2025. The new year looks brighter, according to Truist analyst Michael Ciarmoli.

Friday, he upgraded shares of Lockheed to Buy from Hold and took his price target to $605 a share from $500.

Lockheed stock was up 4% at $539.19 in midday trading, while the S&P 500 and Dow Jones Industrial Average were up 0.5% and 0.3%, respectively.

"We believe Lockheed Martin's 2025 underperformance, coupled with attractive valuation, creates a compelling risk/reward profile in 2026," wrote Ciarmoli.

Coming into the week, Lockheed stock was up about 3% over the past 12 months, underperforming the iShares Aerospace & Defense ETF by about 56 percentage points.

There were a few reasons for that, which started shortly after Tesla CEO and one-time Department of Government Efficiency head Elon Musk criticized manned fighter jets. The Lockheed F-35 is the premier manned fighter jet in the world, and accounts for about 25% of total company revenue.

There were also some write-downs for classified programs. The stock was whipsawed a few times by changing defense policies that threaten F-35 international sales and Lockheed's ability to pay dividends.

Many of those fears have subsided, added Ciarmoli. "We anticipate improved execution and operational performance in 2026...Ongoing geopolitical tensions should continue to drive demand, supporting program production ramp-ups and new capabilities coming online."

The fears, however, have left Lockheed stock trading for about 18 times earnings expected over the coming 12 months, a 25% discount to other large defense prime contractors.

A low valuation can be an opportunity, but fears continue to affect Ciarmoli's peers. Overall, 32% of analysts covering Lockheed stock rate share Buy, according to FactSet. The average Buy-rating ratio for stocks in the S&P 500 is about 55%. The average analyst price target for Lockheed stock is about $535, a little below where shares were trading on Friday.

Write to Al Root at allen.root@dowjones.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Build NYC to Sell $634.2M of Bonds for Bronx Independent Living Facility
data.description:
By Zaeem Shoaib

Build NYC Resource Corp. plans to sell $634.2 million of bonds that will help fund an independent living facility for a continuing care retirement community in New York.

The issuance consists of $310.2 million of Series 2026 A Bonds, $65 million of Series 2026 B-1 Mandatory Paydown Revenue Securities and $87 million of Series 2026 B-2 Mandatory Paydown Revenue Securities, according to documents posted Thursday on MuniOS.

In addition, the sale includes $161 million of Series 2026 B-3 Mandatory Paydown Revenue Securities and $11 million of Series 2026 C Revenue Bonds.

All other securities being issued are tax-exempt, except the Series 2026C bonds.

Interest on the bonds will be payable on June 15 and Dec. 15.

Build NYC Resource is a not-for-profit local development corporation.

Proceeds of the bonds will be loaned to RiverSpring Health Senior Living for financing the construction, renovation, furnishing, and equipping of an approximately 441,000 square foot independent living facility for a continuing care retirement community in the Bronx, New York.

RiverSpring, a New York not-for-profit corporation created to develop and operate a continuing care retirement community, will also use issuance proceeds for the demolition of an existing building located on an adjacent leased parcel of land and the construction of certain improvements

The community operator will also use the funds for the re-payment of pre-construction financing for development costs and repayment of funds advanced by an affiliate for pre-construction and development costs.

The bonds are not rated.

B.C. Ziegler & Co. and Herbert J. Sims & Co. are lead managers for the issuance.

Write to Zaeem Shoaib at zaeem.shoaib@wsj.com

data.title: Democratic senators demand Apple, Google take X and Grok off app stores over sexual images
data.description:
Three Democratic U.S. senators are calling on Apple and Alphabet's Google to remove X and its built-in chabot Grok from their respective app stores over the spread of nonconsensual sexual images of women and minors on the platform.

In a letter published Friday, senators Ron Wyden of Oregon, Ben Ray Lujan of New Mexico, and Edward Markey of Massachusetts said Google and Apple "must remove these apps from the app stores until X’s policy violations are addressed."

Google and Apple didn't immediately return messages seeking comment. X did not immediately return a message.

The letter was first reported by NBC News.

data.title: Hassett: High Productivity Doesn't Have to Mean Huge Job Numbers — Market Talk
data.description: As markets navigate an atypical situation of softer job growth alongside strong GDP numbers, National Economic Council Director Kevin Hassett says that the productivity boom resolves that disconnect in an interview with CNBC. "Productivity is through the roof," he says. "It doesn't necessarily have to mean a whole huge amount of job creation," he added. Hassett argues that productivity-driven growth doesn't necessarily mean an increase to inflation — and points to conditions in the '90s under Alan Greenspan. "It'll be really, really important for people at the Fed to monitor it and make sure that that story holds, but we're seeing it in the data for sure that we've got really, really high growth, and we don't have accelerating in place," he says. (jessica.coacci@wsj.com; jessica_coacci)

Elixirr International plc (ELIX.L), an established, global, award-winning, challenger consultancy, announces that on 8 January 2026 Amanda Ferguson, wife of Ian Ferguson, Co-Founder and board adviser, sold 2,998 ordinary shares of 0.005 pence each in the capital of the Company ("Ordinary Shares") at a price of 834 pence per share.

About Elixirr International plc

Elixirr is an award-winning global consulting firm working with clients across a diverse range of industries, markets and geographies. Founded in 2009, the firm set out to be the 'challenger consultancy' and do things differently than the large corporate consultancies dominating the industry: working openly and collaboratively with clients from start to finish, delivering outcomes based on innovative thinking, not methodology, and treating each client's business like their own. Elixirr was quoted on the AIM market of the London Stock Exchange in 2020 and listed on the Main Market of the London Stock Exchange in July 2025. In addition to strong organic growth, Elixirr has acquired eight boutique firms - Den Creative, Coast Digital, The Retearn Group, iOLAP, Responsum, Insigniam, Hypothesis and TRC Advisory - to grow the Group's capabilities, diversify the business, expand into new geographies and access new clients.

data.title: Gold set for weekly gain on US payrolls miss, broader uncertainty
data.description:
Key points:
  • US job growth slows in December while unemployment rate eases
  • US Supreme Court ruling on tariffs expected on Wednesday
  • Metal Focus sees gold hitting new record highs above $5000/oz
  • BofA raises 2026 average price forecasts for platinum, palladium

By Anmol Choubey

Gold prices rose on Friday and were on track for a weekly gain, as investors weighed weaker-than-expected U.S. payrolls data along with broader policy and geopolitical uncertainty.

Spot gold was up 0.6% at $4,502.98 per ounce as of 11:18 a.m. ET (1618 GMT), and was set for more than 4% weekly gain. Bullion hit a record high of $4,549.71 on December 26.

U.S. gold futures for February delivery firmed 1.2% to $4,513.60.

U.S. nonfarm payrolls in December rose by 50,000, missing expectations of a 60,000 gain, while the unemployment rate eased to 4.4%, below forecasts of 4.5%.

"Payrolls are showing us a poor job creation environment. Potentially more (geopolitical tension), somewhat higher oil prices, which are inflationary, uncertainty and an easing Fed — all a combination for precious metals," said Bart Melek, global head of commodity strategy at TD Securities.

Market participants continued to factor in at least two Federal Reserve rate cuts this year, a backdrop historically favorable for gold. (FEDWATCH)

Geopolitical tensions remained elevated amid intensifying unrest in Iran, continued fighting in Russia's war in Ukraine, the U.S. capture of Venezuela's President Nicolás Maduro, and Washington's renewed signals over taking control of Greenland.

Metals Focus projected gold prices could hit fresh record highs above $5,000 in 2026, citing de-dollarization trends and geopolitical risks.

Retail demand for gold in India remained subdued due to elevated prices, while gold premiums in China widened.

Meanwhile, uncertainty over tariffs persisted, as the U.S. Supreme Court is not expected to issue a ruling on Friday in a major case testing the legality of President Donald Trump’s sweeping global tariffs, with decisions now expected on Wednesday.

Spot silver gained 3.9% to $79.88 per ounce and was on track to log an about 10% weekly rise.

Spot platinum rose 1.3% to $2,296 per ounce. Palladium climbed 2.3% to $1,826.89 per ounce. Both metals were set for weekly gains as well.

Bank of America raised its 2026 average platinum and palladium price forecasts, citing dislocations from trade disputes amid physical market tightness, while China's imports add support.

data.title: Altria rises after UBS upgrades stock to 'buy'
data.description:
** Shares of tobacco giant Altria Group up ~2% to $57.06

** UBS upgrades stock to "buy" from "neutral", raises PT to $63 from $61

** Says despite slow progress in smoke-free, Altria could return to revenue growth, and deliver an acceleration in EPS growth over next two years

** Says worst of Altria's cigarette volume decline could be behind, citing improvement in 2026 helped by industry trends and price investments

** Brokerage believes cigarette sales are declining more slowly as there are fewer smokers left to switch to vapes

** Six of 16 brokerages rate the stock "buy" or higher, seven "hold", 3 rate the stock "sell" or higher; with a median PT of $62.50

** Shares rose 10.3% in 2025

data.title: Aktis Oncology soars in debut after upsized $318 million IPO
data.description:
** Cancer drug developer Aktis Oncology's (AKTS.O) shares jump as much as 62% in their Nasdaq debut

** Stock opened for trading at $27 apiece vs $18 IPO price, valuing the firm at $1.42 billion

** AKTS late Thursday sold 17.65 million shares in an upsized offering at the top end of its marketed range of $16 and $18 apiece to raise $317.7 million

** Co-founded in August 2020, AKTS is developing treatments for a broad range of solid tumors

** Its lead candidate, AKY-1189, is in an early-stage study to treat certain solid tumors, including a form of advanced bladder cancer, breast cancer and colorectal cancer

** "By going public now, Aktis is positioning itself as the next potential acquisition target in the rapidly growing radiopharmaceutical sector and can therefore justify a premium" - IPOX research associate Lukas Muehlbauer

data.title: EU's South America Trade Deal Shows World Is Moving Away From U.S., Economist Says — Market Talk
data.description: EU member states' approval of the bloc's long-awaited trade deal with four South American countries highlights how governments are seeking to diversify their partnerships away from relying on the U.S, Antonio Fatas, an economics professor at INSEAD, says. "Starting last year, the world has changed and Europe, understand that we need to point in a different direction," he says. The EU's deal with founding Mercosur countries Argentina, Brazil, Paraguay and Uruguay--which has been negotiated on and off for around 25 years--now makes up part of the bloc's effort to wean off its economic reliance on the U.S. and China. "I think this is a statement that the world is moving away from the U.S.," he says. (edith.hancock@wsj.com)
